---
title: "1.5 Casting and Ranges of Values"
subtitle: "Part 1: Casting and Widening Conversion"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Casting

### Convert a value from one type to another

In Java, <span class="term">type casting</span> (类型转换) is used to convert values from one type to another.

By <span class="term">casting</span>, the source means a similar idea to casting a bronze: reshaping something into another form.

In Java, when you cast you are changing the "shape" or type of the value.

## Casting Analogy

### Bronze casting

::: {.image-fit}
![Bronze casting changes the shape of the metal](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-5-casting-and-ranges-of-values/bronze-casting.jpg){fig-align="center" width="36%"}
:::

Bronze casting changes the shape of the metal. Type casting changes the type shape of a value.

## Cast Operators

### `(int)` and `(double)`

The <span class="term">cast operator</span>, which looks like `(int)` and `(double)` placed before any expression, produces a value of the given type.

The casting operators `(int)` and `(double)` can be used to convert from a `double` value to an `int` value or vice versa.

## Casting Examples

### New type, new result

```java
(double) 1 / 3
(int) 3.6
```

::: {.tight-list}
- `(double) 1 / 3` evaluates to a `double` value `0.33333333` instead of an `int` truncated value `0`.
- `(int) 3.6` evaluates to an `int` value `3` instead of a `double`.
- Casting a `double` value to an `int` truncates digits to the right of the decimal point.
:::

## Code Task

### `activecode:: cast-division`

Textbook prompt: What happens when you divide an `int` by an `int`, with a `double` operand, or with the type cast `(double)` or `(int)` on one of the operands?

Add another line that divides `5` by `2` using a `(double)` cast.

What is the result?

## Code Window

### `activecode:: cast-division`

```java
public class Casting
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println(3 / 4);          // int divided by int
        System.out.println(3.0 / 4);        // double divided by int
        System.out.println(3 / 4.0);        // int divided by double
        System.out.println((double) 3 / 4); // int cast to double, divided by int
        System.out.println((int) 3.0 / 4);  // double cast to int, divided by int
        // Add a line of code that prints out the result of dividing 5 by 2
        //  using a (double) cast.

    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: cast-division`

Runestone expects this output from `main` after adding the final line:

```text
0
0.75
0.75
0.75
0
2.5
```

## Truncating Is Not Rounding

### `int / int`

When Java divides two `int`s, it produces an `int` result by truncating the actual mathematical result, removing anything after the decimal point.

Thus `9 / 10` evaluates to `0`, not `0.9`.

It also does not evaluate to `1`; truncating is not the same as rounding.

## Widening Conversion

### `double` is contagious

In any expression involving a `double`, the `double` is "contagious" and will cause the value of that expression to also be a `double`.

Thus `9.0 / 10` is evaluated as if it had been written:

```java
9.0 / 10.0
```

and produces the `double` value `0.9`.

## Widening Conversion

### `int` to `double`

This code causes `int` values like `10` to be automatically cast, or widened, to `double` values like `10.0`.

Casting an `int` to `double` produces a `double` value, which will then cause any expression it is part of to produce a `double`.

A conversion from `int` to `double` is called a <span class="term">widening conversion</span> because a `double` can represent any `int` value but not vice versa.

## Average Pattern

### Cast before division

This is especially useful when you have `int` variables that you want to do non-integer division with:

```java
int total; // a variable containing the sum of a bunch of ints
int count; // the number of ints that went into total

// Compute the average of the bunch of ints summed into total.
double average = (double) total / count;
```

## `int` Range Note

### Signed values and constants

`int`s in Java are always 32-bit signed values.

They can represent values from `-2^31` to `2^31 - 1`, inclusive.

The minimum and maximum `int` values can be referenced with:

```java
Integer.MIN_VALUE
Integer.MAX_VALUE
```

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- define <span class="term">type casting</span> as converting values from one type to another
- identify `(int)` and `(double)` as cast operators
- explain that casting `double` to `int` truncates digits after the decimal point
- explain why `int / int` truncates rather than rounds
- explain widening conversion from `int` to `double`
- use `(double) total / count` to preserve the fractional part of an average
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 2

[Next: Rounding, ranges, and precision →](1-5-part-2-rounding-ranges-and-precision.html)
